speaker: interviewer
text: Can you count please from one to 10 so I can check your mic?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks. Sounds good. Doris?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date, September 20, 1995. Interview with Bluma Weinstock. The interviewer is Doris Lazarus. The interview is taking place in Bayside, Wisconsin, USA, and is being conducted in English. September 20, 1995. I'm Doris Lazarus conducting an interview with Bluma Weinstock in Bayside, Wisconsin. Please begin by stating your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am Bluma Weinstock.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell your last name please?

speaker: interviewee
text: W-E-I-N-S-T-O-C-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: They called me Bluma.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pelman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: P-E-L-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go by any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They called me home [INAUDIBLE] Bluma. And they call me here the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: 19-- 20 November, 1919.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: 74. That's right. I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city and country were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm born in Poland, in city of Radomsko.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what you remember about the city that you grew up in?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know [? bad there ?]. We was happy there. As children, we played outside. Just-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] [CRYING] Makes me cry. When I was six years old, my mother passed away. And it was very hard for me till today. And then my-- my father remarried, and then he got other three children. Before that, my mother was married, and she got a son. And my father got-- was married, and he got a son too. And they both married. They got two children, then I was born. My mother's first husband, he was in-- in the army, 1914. And he-- he lost his leg, and he died. And-- and she came later with-- with her son. And she went to her-- her sister. Her sister lived near Radomsko. And she was very good, well off. And she-- they told-- they told me the story. And she lived there [INAUDIBLE], I don't know how long. And my father got a son because he's [INAUDIBLE]. And his child was born, [? with ?] his son. And then they married, and I was born. And I have a younger brother. He survived too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Esther.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when your father remarried, what was your stepmother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her-- her name was Fella. They called her [INAUDIBLE]. And then-- then, like a-- like a-- a stepmother, I grew up. I got all the-- I remember we used to have like a maid in the house when my mother was living. Then she said, you're grown up. We don't need it. And I have to do the work because I was-- so it was-- and then my father-- I've [? forgotten-- ?] married my mother, the two, my brothers, they-- one-- both left the house. She said she doesn't want to have so many children at home. One went to my auntie, which my mother came the first time-- and my other brother went to his-- our uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the two brothers was not home, just I was with my brother. And then, after the years, she got another three children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I-- I-- I have to do all the work at home, like Cinderella. And then we-- I finished school. I finished seventh grade. This was-- to college in Europe was very hard to go. I didn't got no opportunity just-- and I start to learn to be a [? seam make. ?] [INAUDIBLE]. And then mine-- my stepmother, we got a food store for all kinds of food like you see here, from all-- all the other countries. And the-- we-- I think they make the beautiful living that time. Just my auntie, my mother's sister, used to come in the house. And my brother, which she stayed-- my brother stayed with them, they always came in the house and they're so-- they wasn't happy that I washing dishes or peeling potatoes or raising her children. I raised all the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she said we-- that I want to live no more in Radomsko. Then-- and we moved to Sosnowiec, and this was maybe two years before the war came out. We moved to Sosnowiec. In Sosnowiec, my father didn't make a good living no more.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that he [INAUDIBLE] why they moved. And they-- and they always said because of me, because of my relative, they came in the house. We-- they have to move. She couldn't stand no more that my brother, my auntie, they're coming in the house to check on me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- I-- I looked for a job. It was very hard, but I find a job. And I-- I worked by sewing. How old were you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I started to work, maybe-- this was before the war, 17, 18, something like that. And I liked always clothes, and I-- but I make some money. I like to buy a nice coat and nice clothes. You know, but not so easy to-- to make something. Just in-- and then the world breaked up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any happy memories of your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I-- then came vacation time right in summer. My auntie invited me to come to Radomsko. I should stay with her because I loved that city because I was born and all my friends was there. And-- and I begged her. I want to stay by her. And she said, no, I cannot stay because you have a father. You should stay by-- by your father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a relationship did you have with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, they say in Jewish that said [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. He was-- when I complained something, he said, you know, this is my third wife. Should I divorce her? I cannot do it no more, because I complain, she's not good to me. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were growing up, were you've given a Jewish education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I-- I-- remember I went to-- yeah, we learned Jewish separately from school, from that one school separately. We-- I went like in-- they call it a cheider. And we learned three, four times in the week. We learned, not especially, just to know how to pray in Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was after school.

speaker: interviewee
text: After school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: A public school. It was-- actually, it was all Jewish children. It was-- it was not a Jewish school. Just, it was a public school which all Jewish children went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have many non-Jews in the neighborhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, just-- just Jewish, Jewish friends, Jewish neighbors. Yeah. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about in business, did your father do business with Jews as well as non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. We got a open store for food, all kinds of food. And they came, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any type of anti-Semitism when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe later on. As a child, we didn't know. Later on before the war, we-- we first saw anti-Semitism. They put signs not to buy by Jews, with-- not to go with them. You know, it was like anti-Semitic.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children were there now in your family, including stepbrothers and stepsisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was seven. My father got one son. My mother got-- oh, but they went away, and then my-- me and my brother. And then she got other three children, wonderful children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me the names of your brothers and sisters and maybe something that you remember about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my oldest brother, my mother's son, he-- he was also good to me. He was like a father cannot be like. He was good to me. He-- but, you know, they told me the story of my mother. Then she married. She lived not far away from [INAUDIBLE]. This shtetl [INAUDIBLE]. It was [INAUDIBLE]. And she-- they have a house there. And this house was written for my brother. And he always said to me that if I get married-- he-- he was, at the time, young, and he couldn't sign for it. He then finished his army. He was going to sign that's going to be for me. He doesn't need money. But I'm a-- a girl. I'm going to need money, he said. He was very good to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Moishe. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in the-- in the Polish army. He probably got killed from the German because there was-- they had start to fight. And he got killed, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the next child after him?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the next child was my father's son, Peretz. And he-- actually, with him, I was not too much because we went away, another city. And my brother, [? Moishe, ?] he was in the same city where I was. So I remember the most from my mother's, my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your brother Peretz.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's alive. He's in Milwaukee, and he's alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was born next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next, me, my brother?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jack is his name. And I was older from him, about six years older. And he didn't know my mother because he was a child. When my mother passed away, he was maybe two months old. So then my father remarried, and he got three children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to Jack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jack is alive. He's here. The two brothers I have here, Peretz and Jack. They're in Milwaukee.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then your father had three more children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. One is-- do you want the name? One was Chanaleh, and one was Sureleh, and one was, like, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to his three children?

speaker: interviewee
text: They went [? all ?] to Auschwitz together with my father and with my stepmother. They all went to Auschwitz. So I-- in-- when the war breaked up, I-- it was just terrible. The-- the-- you know, the SS came in middle the nights. They look for young boys or girls to take in the camp. And I was hiding every time in another place, because I was afraid. And then I-- they opened a shop. They're called [INAUDIBLE] shop in [NON-ENGLISH]. And I tried to get a summer job, and I got a job there. We make the shirt for the Gestapo, the brown shirts they make. And I was like a-- I pressed, like, I ironed the shirt. When the shirt was finished, they came up and I ironed. And I don't remember. Maybe I worked about two years or a year and a half. I don't remember how long. And they closed up the shop. And they took me to the-- like a [INAUDIBLE]. You know, you have to be [INAUDIBLE] all from the shop. And the-- and I went to the Szopienice camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you went to this camp--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you said that you worked in the factory for about two years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I pressed those-- those shirts for the Germans. This was for the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was happening in your town at that point to most of the Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was terrible. [SIGHS] First of all, the food we didn't got-- we got-- we'd get coupons. And you have to stay in the line for days, nights. I remember that for bread, let's see, we have to-- I have to wake up, and it was middle the night, like 2 o'clock, to stay through the morning and called and arranged to wait, still we could get from the bakery and the coupon, the bread. And we came the end, someplace they said they're out of it. And it was just terrible. Then they took my brother, my father. They have to go out and work outside, like sweeping the streets. And I remember in Sosnowiec, I-- they hanged some people. I don't know what they did, probably didn't do nothing wrong. They hanged-- they hanged them out. And we have to go-- everybody have to go out to see them hanging. And it was a torture. It was a torture. And then I went to the-- to-- then they catch me from the-- from that [INAUDIBLE] for what I was ironing. They closed up, and they took me to the camp they called Szopienice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: This camp was not far away from Sosnowiec, maybe a half hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you go with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With-- with all the people which they catch that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anybody from your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was the first only one which I went to camp. Later on, I heard my brother, Jack, for this year, he went to camp too, another camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they took you away at this time, was that the last time that you saw your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think that it was going to be the last time you saw them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was thinking, yeah, I will never see them again. No. And in Szopienice, it was-- actually, the-- we [? was ?] there, maybe 20 girls. And we worked, and we peeled potatoes-- we prepared food for the kitchen. It was a big barrack. And we sit there. We peel potatoes. We-- we cut cabbage. And in-- in that-- the most there was there from Holland. They brought a lot of people from Holland, mens. And they went every day to work, and every day they came less from the work. And came a Sunday, let's say-- Sunday, there was like the block fuhrer. He took out some mens, and he put hoses in his mouth with water. He put-- they have so long to [INAUDIBLE]. We have to all stay there and see-- and see this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and-- every time was less-- less people there. And then they closed up our shop, that-- the camp. How were you treated when you were working at this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was not-- the girls, we didn't got too bad. We got potatoes because we worked with the potatoes and cabbage. So we didn't got-- at that time, we could have our hair. They didn't cut the hair. That camp for the girls, for us, was very good. Just the mens that got so terrible. They-- every day there was more dead. They closed up. It was just not left many. And they took us on buses, and we went to Auschwitz from that camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you ever heard of Auschwitz at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We know when I was in Sosnowiec. They said, if somebody goes to Auschwitz, nobody will come back. They-- they killing them right away. They're putting-- we-- we-- we heard all this, that they're put in gas chambers. So we didn't know from-- when we were in Szopienice, we go to Auschwitz. They say, we go to another camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came to Auschwitz. [SIGHS] It was very-- first, they put you in barracks. And they take away everything what you had. In Szopienice, we still could wear our clothes, what we got, even we didn't took too many with us because--

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- and they shaved your hair. And we looked at each other. We couldn't recognize. Everybody looked unbelievable. And then we-- we was there in a quarantine. And they didn't put you to work yet. You had to stay there-- I don't remember how long it was. And-- [DRINKS]. And we-- all my-- maybe we was there about a month, six weeks, something like that. And all my friends got sick on typhus. And everybody almost died. And I know-- and I got sick too. And I knew I-- every day that's going to happen to me. And I got so sick. And I think I and another girl, she survived too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and I-- I-- I was so sick with such high temperature that I wanted a drink and nobody comes to you. And I think the whole block was like that, you know, who was there? Hundreds, it was maybe two, three hundreds always was in-- in that block.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was middle of the night, but I remember. And it was very cold, maybe it was-- it was snow-- it was snow on the ground. And then the night I said to myself, I hadn't got anybody to talk, and I said I have to go down to a washroom and drink water as much I can. And later, I-- I-- I can die. I don't want to live because we-- I know we're not going to live. And I went to a washroom. I drank so much water, and then I said to myself, I go to the electric wires because [CRYING] you was all sick. You didn't know what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went, and I wanted-- and you touched-- the whole camp was with electric wires. They-- they told us right away, if you go to those wires, you get electrified and die right away. And I was thinking, this is going to be the best for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the top was a-- a-- a tower. Yeah, around the camp was towers. And the-- it was not a gestapo. I think this was regular soldiers there. And how I went to the-- to the electric wire was a hill like a little river. You have to cross to go, and I want to go. And that-- and that-- it's so painful-- and that soldier, the Germans start to holler me. I should-- I should go away because he gonna shoot at me, would want one to kill me. Then I said to him, I want to be killed. I don't want to live no more. I'm sick. I'm not gonna live. And then he start to shoot, not on me, on the air. I should get scared, not to go close, and he start to call a kapo. It was in the night. There was a kapo outside, which there was looking around. He start to call her, and she came to me, and she start to holler. But I-- I told her that I'm not gonna live, and I cannot live, that I'm so sick. You know, [INAUDIBLE], and you-- you're so sick. You have typhus. And he said, he should ask me which block I'm in. She take me back to the block. And-- and she took me to the-- to the block, and it was inside. This was the middle the night. They waked her up, and she said that I wanted-- I was thinking she gonna kill me, that-- that kapo. This was a Jewish kapo, and they live in a little room.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she-- she start to holler [INAUDIBLE]. I said, you see, all we-- we came all died already, and I'm not gonna live either. And then she-- I was thinking, is she gonna kill me? She said, no. She was nice. She gave me a glass of milk.

speaker: interviewee
text: She gave me a blanket. And I laid down. I should go lay down. And after this, I start-- I started to get better in the morning. I start to feel better. So-- and then debating, debate for a while, and they said-- she came to me, that kapo, all the time, to see how I am. And  she see that I start to feel better. And she said, the SS will come, probably, in a week. And they're gonna-- not just me, the other people too, they're gonna check you out. And they're gonna send you to the-- to the working camp. Now I was in the quarantine.
